text: The surgeon has entered the abdomen through the abdominal ports, revealing the gallbladder with significant adhesions to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses one dissector to lift the liver while employing another dissector to peel off the adherent omentum from the gallbladder, which has thick stones and limited space at the fundus.
The surgeon carefully approaches the gallbladder, seeking a cleavage plane for dissection, staying close to the gallbladder to avoid surrounding structures.
The surgeon encounters fibrous adhesions around the gallbladder, which have developed from fibrinous adhesions over time, complicating the dissection process.
The surgeon carefully separates adhesions to reveal the gallbladder, which is initially obscured by these fibrous tissue bands.
The surgeon attempts to grasp the gallbladder, which appears visibly thickened, making it difficult to hold.
The surgeon uses forceps to push back tissue, opening the forceps to manipulate the area around the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses forceps to carefully find a location near the gallbladder to separate the dense adhesions surrounding it.
The surgeon identifies the gallbladder, visually confirming its presence in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum located below the gallbladder in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the proximity of the duodenum to the surgical field, highlighting the risk of injury with sharp or careless movements during dissection.

The surgeon approaches the fundus of the gallbladder, moving past the body towards Hartmann's pouch, the lower portion of the gallbladder, while ensuring there is no cholecysto-duodenal fistula present.
The surgeon gently manipulates the gallbladder fundus, which has been pushed back to a superior position relative to the liver, ensuring careful adherence to surgical principles to avoid harm.
The surgeon uses one hand to apply pressure on the gallbladder while the other hand carefully maneuvers within the cleavage plane to separate tissue, likely identifying and avoiding the duodenum.
The surgeon gradually exposes more of the gallbladder, revealing its structure step by step as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon separates adhesions around the gallbladder, revealing more of the organ as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon carefully removes some serosa from the gallbladder, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the instruments to closely follow the contour of the gallbladder, ensuring precise dissection around its surface.
The surgeon uses a Maryland curved dissector to closely follow the contour of the gallbladder, adhering to the rule of 'hugging' the gallbladder during dissection.
The surgeon gently grasps the surrounding tissue with a surgical instrument and pulls it downward, carefully manipulating the tissue adjacent to the gallbladder.

The surgeon dissects adhesions causing the falciform ligament, normally located in the midline, to be pulled towards the right side. As dissection progresses, the ligament gradually returns to its original position.
The surgeon carefully dissects the serosa of the gallbladder, ensuring that the duodenum remains undamaged during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the Hartmann's pouch of the gallbladder, which becomes increasingly clear.
The Hartmann's pouch is visible, gradually tapering down into the cystic duct.
The surgeon dissects thick fibrous adhesions on the lateral right side, using bipolar coagulation to manage potential vessels within the tissue, ensuring a clear surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the junction of the gallbladder with the cystic duct but decides to dissect above this junction, respecting the tissue to ensure a clean dissection.
The surgeon performs a retrograde dissection under the body of the gallbladder, working within the gallbladder bed between the gallbladder and the liver.
The surgeon creates a cleavage plane between the gallbladder and the liver, aiming to extend this plane further downwards.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection between the cystic duct, gallbladder, common bile duct, and cystic artery, creating a separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, clearly exposing the cystic duct and cystic artery for safe dissection.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, clearly visualizing the cystic artery and cystic duct, with up to one-third of the gallbladder dissected from the gallbladder bed.

The surgeon navigates through a safe area, carefully dissecting around the thickened and fibrotic gallbladder wall, which shows signs of previous inflammation. The laparoscopic approach is used to progress through the challenging tissue.
The surgeon attempts to grasp the slippery gallbladder tissue using a blunt grasper but decides to switch to a tooth grasper for better control.
The surgeon uses the left hand to find a cleavage plane between the gallbladder and the liver bed, indicating the progression of dissection.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the cleavage plane on the anterior or left side of the gallbladder, carefully separating the tissue layers.

The surgeon carefully navigates fibrotic areas around the gallbladder, identifying the posterior cystic branch while distinguishing between artery and fibrosis. Progress is made slowly and gradually, with small, deliberate movements to avoid damage.
The surgeon gradually frees the gallbladder from its attachment to the liver, allowing it to be moved right and left. The posterior side of the gallbladder is visible as it becomes more mobile.
The surgeon attempts to approach the gallbladder from the left or anterior side, maneuvering to further free it from the liver attachment.

The surgeon pauses the dissection to check for bleeding and old clots, as some bleeding is inevitable due to extensive dissection. The gallbladder is being separated from adhesions along the liver, with the duodenum already pushed down.
The surgeon gently pushes downwards to identify the cystic duct, distinguishing it from the gallbladder, while exercising caution during the dissection.

The surgeon demonstrates the anatomical connection of the cystic duct entering the gallbladder and its junction with the common bile duct and common hepatic duct, ensuring clear visualization of these structures.
The surgeon continues to remove adhesions from the gallbladder, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues, including the liver.
The surgeon carefully cuts the fascia surrounding the gallbladder, using gentle finger movements to expose the diseased organ with precision.
The surgeon carefully scrapes tissue between the cystic duct and the gallbladder, managing some bleeding while maintaining gentle manipulation of the area.

The surgeon carefully dissects the fascia attached on the inferior or right side, creating a space while being cautious of the posterior branch of the cystic artery that may be present within the fascia.
The surgeon observes bleeding and tissue spread while manipulating the gallbladder, highlighting the need for careful handling to prevent severe bleeding.
The surgeon identifies a posterior branch of the cystic artery, which appears slightly peeled off from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon leaves a dissected area and begins dissection from the opposite end to bring the cystic artery into view, carefully navigating the anatomical structures.

The surgeon clears the fascia from above, revealing the cystic artery, which becomes more visible. The anatomical variations of the gallbladder are noted, including the potential for the artery to originate from posterior or anterior positions and variations in the cystic duct's length and thickness.
The surgeon resumes dissection from above to delineate the cystic artery, carefully clearing the fascia to improve visibility of the artery.
The surgeon continues the dissection close to the gallbladder, carefully working to delineate the artery while managing minor bleeding in the area.
The surgeon carefully dissects the fascia below the gallbladder, taking care to avoid the common hepatic duct, which is in close proximity.

The surgeon carefully dissects around small arteries near the cystic duct, ensuring the dissector does not inadvertently enter the common hepatic duct. The small arteries are identified as potentially belonging to the cystic duct rather than the cystic artery.
The surgeon addresses small bleeding by applying pressure using the gallbladder or a gauze piece from outside the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls the gallbladder upward from the fossa to enhance the view and clear the quadrangle, revealing surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon clears the quadrangle to improve visibility, aiding in the delineation of the cystic artery and cystic duct.
The surgeon continues to elevate the gallbladder from the fossa, enhancing the view of the surgical field to better delineate the cystic artery and duct.
The surgeon removes adhesions between the gallbladder and the liver bed, applying pressure to the right field to facilitate the separation of these structures.

The surgeon carefully peels off the fascia surrounding the gallbladder, ensuring the common hepatic duct is safely pushed down. The fascia is removed from near the gallbladder, with the gallbladder visible above and the common hepatic duct positioned below, eventually joining the cystic duct to form the common bile duct.
The surgeon uses a tooth grasper to hold the gallbladder, gently separating fibrous threads posteriorly and then moving anteriorly or to the left side.
The surgeon observes the gallbladder, noting its thickened appearance.
The surgeon observes the gallbladder wall thickness, measuring close to one centimeter, which is unlikely to tear even when manipulated with a tooth grasper.
The surgeon has made openings in the gallbladder, revealing the interior structure.

The surgeon has reached the inferior and right side between the gallbladder and liver bed, now proceeding superiorly and to the left. The fascia is clearly visible, and the common hepatic duct is seen below, safely positioned.
The surgeon revisits the dissection between the gallbladder and the liver fossa, ensuring the critical view of safety is maintained.
The surgeon works to enhance the critical view of safety by carefully delineating vital structures between the gallbladder and liver fossa.
The surgeon carefully separates the cystic duct from the cystic artery, which is visible posteriorly, using dissection techniques within the Calot's triangle.
The surgeon identifies the common hepatic duct and common bile duct, noting their positions relative to each other.
The cystic duct is identified above the gallbladder.
The surgeon separates fibrous tissue from the cystic artery, carefully delineating the artery from surrounding structures.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, clearly displaying the cystic duct and cystic artery as distinct structures.
The surgeon achieves a clear corridor angle in the critical view of safety, with visible separation of the cystic duct and artery from surrounding fibrous tissue.

The surgeon identifies a small vessel, likely the posterior branch of the cystic artery, which is bleeding. A clip is applied to the tip of the vessel to control the bleeding, noting visible pulsation.
The surgeon applies a clip to a vessel showing pulsation to prevent future bleeding, ensuring it is securely clipped.

The surgeon applies the first clip on the cystic duct, ensuring it is pressed for some time to displace inflammatory tissue fluid, reducing the risk of clip slippage. The cystic artery and cystic duct are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies clips on the cystic duct, visibly relaxing the duct as the clips are secured.
The surgeon pulls up the gallbladder to facilitate the application of clips on the side of the gallbladder, ensuring proper positioning for secure attachment.
The surgeon avoids applying clips close to the junction of the common bile duct, common hepatic duct, and cystic duct, ensuring precise placement to prevent complications.

The surgeon cuts the cystic artery after confirming its pulsation, with clips securely placed on the cystic artery. A small blood collection is suctioned out, and the gallbladder is separated from the gallbladder fossa for removal.
The surgeon uses cautery to separate the remaining portion of the gallbladder from the fossa, addressing strong adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon positions the cautery device close to the gallbladder to continue the dissection process.
The surgeon carefully hugs the gallbladder to avoid damaging surrounding liver tissue, while being mindful of potential venous sinuses or bile ducts beneath the dissection area.
The surgeon applies cautery to the gallbladder, resulting in increased bleeding from the surgical site.
The surgeon maintains the electrocautery device close to the gallbladder, carefully navigating around the structure to avoid damaging underlying liver tissue or venous sinuses.
The surgeon observes a gallbladder with a notably thick wall, indicating its distinct appearance during the procedure.
The surgeon applies cautery close to the gallbladder, carefully identifying the cleavage plane between the gallbladder and the liver.
